caption:
PCOS just got a new name. Here’s what it means for you.

After 11 years of global research and 22,000+ survey responses, experts officially renamed “polycystic ovary syndrome” to PMOS: Polyendocrine Metabolic Ovarian Syndrome.

Why? Because the old name was wrong.
There are no abnormal cysts. The “polycystic” appearance on ultrasound is actually just follicles — completely normal ovarian activity. And the condition was never just an ovary problem. It involves insulin, androgens, neuroendocrine hormones, metabolism, and cardiovascular risk.

The new name reflects what the science has been saying for years: this is a whole-body, multi-hormone, metabolic condition. Not a cyst problem.

Here’s what each word means: 
→ Polyendocrine: multiple hormones are disrupted — not just estrogen and progesterone 
→ Metabolic: insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes risk, heart disease, and fatty liver are finally acknowledged 
→ Ovarian: broader than “cysts” — captures hormonal changes without implying something is structurally wrong

A better name means better awareness, faster diagnosis, and more complete care.

3 heart health labs not on the standard panel everyone should know.

1. Apolipoprotein B (ApoB) - ApoB tells you the total number of atherogenic particles in your bloodstream. Every LDL, VLDL, IDL, and Lp(a) particle carries one ApoB molecule.

2. Lipoprotein (a) - is a genetically inherited risk factor for cardiovascular disease. Roughly 1 in 5 people have elevated Lp(a). Most people never get tested.

3. C Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) - is an indication of your general inflammation levels. Inflammation is a known driver for many diseases.

Peptides are having their moment. But are they the secret to living longer or just the internet’s latest obsession?

Some peptides show potential in supporting tissue repair, recovery, and metabolism. But many of the therapies going viral today are still early in research, and the long-term data just isn’t there yet.

Meanwhile, the things that truly extend lifespan are the basics: sleep, nutrition, muscle, stress regulation, and metabolic stability. These fundamentals are backed by decades of evidence and are far more established than most emerging tools.

So before exploring anything new, ask yourself: What’s your real goal? And is there a safer, more proven path to get there?

More often than not, the answer is yes and it may or may not involve peptides at all.

On one of the spookiest days of the year, fear takes center stage. But we’re not talking about what’s hiding in the dark. We’re talking about what’s hidden in plain sight: your health.

What’s building secretly inside you.

Heart disease, cancer, hormone decline, nutrient deficiencies. They all go unnoticed for years and build slowly, long before you feel any symptoms. And what may just seem like minor imbalances today can later develop into future diagnoses that put your health at risk.

That’s why early detection matters. Most chronic diseases are preventable if you’re able to catch it early. That’s the difference.

The unseen is only scary until you face it. Get ahead of disease, deficiency, and decline and take the fear out of your health journey.

Every November, the NYC Marathon reminds us what the human body is truly capable of – 26.2 miles of endurance, discipline, and power working in sync. But here’s the thing: you don’t need to run 26.2 miles to test your endurance.

Every day, your body is running its own version of a marathon, from deadlines, workouts, kids, commutes, and stress.

The difference between those who keep going and those who burn out? It’s how efficiently the body produces energy, manages inflammation, and recovers.

When these systems work in sync, your body performs like a well-trained athlete: steady, focused, and resilient.

But when fatigue hits you midday or it’s getting hard to concentrate at work, that’s your system signaling imbalances and that your cells are working overtime to keep up.

Noticing more hair going down the drain and a ponytail that feels much skinnier than last year?

Hair is one of the body’s most metabolically active tissues, meaning it depends on a steady supply of nutrients and energy. When that supply dips, often from stress, hormonal shifts, or nutrient gaps, your follicles are typically the first to feel it.

Before reaching for another supplement or hair serum, start with what’s on your plate. Protein, iron, zinc, omega-3s and vitamin D are the raw materials your body uses to grow healthy strands and food delivers them best.

But if you do need to supplement, do it smarter based on your own body’s data not trends.

Twice a year, we reset our clocks. And with every clock change, we’re disrupting the circadian rhythm our bodies have worked hard to maintain. So if this time change has left you feeling a little off, you’re not imagining it.

When we “spring forward”, research shows higher rates of heart attacks, strokes, and fatigue. Fortunately, this fall season, we’ve set our clocks backs which is far less disruptive to our health as we’re giving our bodies more exposure to morning light and a chance to realign with our natural rhythm.

More light in the morning means steadier energy, better sleep, and fewer risks for cardiovascular strain. This makes us consider how truly important sunlight is for our health.

Your body leaves behind clues long before disease comes into the picture. Subtle changes in your energy, mood, sleep, or focus are not all random, in fact, it could be your body sending signals.

Before it becomes heart disease, thyroid dysfunction, diabetes, or cancer, it’s often small imbalances in your biomarkers that are silently building.

And we’re not saying these are all the definite causes of each conditions, but we’ve seen the patterns firsthand with the patients at Mito. The thing that does hold true every time is that  chronic conditions don’t just happen suddenly, they’ve been building this whole time.

Early detection is your best bet. Because once you know what’s happening inside your body, you can change the outcome through the right supplements, nutrition, and lifestyle shifts.

Test early. Intervene smarter. Live longer.

I thought I was healthy.

That’s how most of our members start their journey with us. But when their results came back, they discovered hidden deficiencies, early risk factors, and patterns no routine checkup had ever shown.

This is a reminder that healthy can look fine on the outside but completely different on the inside.

HRV has become one of the most tracked health metrics because of wearables, used to guide recovery, training, and sleep. But if you’re eating right, sleeping well, and training consistently yet your HRV doesn’t budge despite these strong habits, it often points to something deeper.

It’s not about your habits anymore. It’s what’s happening inside your body.

Your HRV reflects how well your body can respond to stress, recover, and maintain balance. When it plateaus, it’s often a sign your recovery systems are under strain: nutrient deficiencies, elevated cortisol, or reduced cellular efficiency can all contribute.

That’s why HRV should never be looked at in isolation. Your wearable can only show you a number, but deeper bloodwork testing can help explain the “why”.

Magnesium just entered the prostate cancer conversation, and the data is hard to ignore. This 2025 study provides an important new perspective in prostate cancer research, suggesting magnesium depletion could be strongly associated with higher prostate cancer risk.

Testing for magnesium deficiency early could be the simplest first step in protecting long-term health.

Cancer is no longer an “old” person’s disease anymore. Younger adults are seeing rising rates, as of recently.

But research shows what you eat, drink, and do every day can dramatically shift your cancer risk. Up to 40% of cancers are preventable through lifestyle and early detection.

At Mito, we’re making prevention measurable through our Galleri Multi-Cancer Earlt Detection Test that helps you catch signals of over 50+ cancer types in one blood draw.

The sooner you test, the earlier you can act.

You might notice it every time the seasons change, the low energy, the lack of motivation, the loss of joy in things that usually made you feel good.

It’s easy to blame it on the shorter days or colder weather.
But for some, it’s deeper than that. For some, it’s genetic.

The MTHFR gene.
A silent variant carried by nearly 40% of people, most without even knowing it.

When this gene isn’t functioning properly, your body struggles to metabolize folate which is a nutrient essential for producing serotonin and dopamine, the very neurotransmitters that regulate mood and motivation.

That means even if you’re doing everything right like eating well, sleeping enough, exercising, you might still feel off.

Discover if you have the MTHFR variant and learn how to support your body from the inside out.

Our Co-Founder and Chief Medical Officer, Dr. Ryan Ware, will be breaking down 4 common health myths making the rounds on the internet to reveal what the science really says about optimizing your health and longevity.

The flu season is upon us and while you can’t control the germs around you, you can control how prepared your body is to fight back.

Measure your immune system to know if your defenses are ready and supported for the upcoming season. From inflammation to recovery markers to nutrient levels, see how your body is functioning and whether it needs support.

Your exposure is rising, make sure your defenses are too.

We’ve all heard it before - move more, eat better, sleep more. But hardly anyone tells you where to begin or how to know if it’s even working for your body.

Health advice is everywhere, but your body is unique. What may work for one person’s routine may not align with your lifestyle. 

We’ve broken down three simple, feasible habits anyone can benefit from for better sleep, less stress, and daily movement.

But the best way to know what other habits are actually supporting you or silently working against you is through testing. Your blood holds answers that can help you build a routine that moves your health forward and helps you see measurable progress.

64% of our members discovered elevated health risks through their baseline test - and were able to reduce their risk of future disease with early intervention and lifestyle changes. 

The key to preventing disease as you age is early detection so you can make smarter choices before symptoms appear.

These are quiet wins your future health will thank you for.

You don’t feel muscle loss, but it’s happening quietly. Muscle loss doesn’t just begin at old age, it actually starts after your 30s and you’re losing up to 8% of it every decade.

And this shift isn’t just affecting your strength or physique, it changes how your body ages, regulates energy, and recovers. In fact, it’s one of the strongest predictors of early death as declining muscle mass single-handedly raises your risk of diabetes, heart disease, osteoporosis, cognitive decline, and frailty.

While muscle loss is inevitable, how much you lose isn’t. That’s up to you. A DEXA scan helps you stay ahead of it.

He looks 34.
His biology says otherwise.

Tyler trains, eats well, and even looks like he does everything “right”. But on the inside, his body is working overtime.

Hormonal imbalances and deficiencies that may seem minor now could be silently building and accelerating aging beneath the surface. 

A simple blood draw can catch it early and adjust your future.
